Details: As indicated from batch isotherm tests, adsorption can be very effective (up to 99 percent removal) for mercury removal. Data for full-scale water treatment were not available.  Mercury removal by adsorptive media is largely dependent on the initial concentration, oxidation state, adsorbent type, and water chemistry (pH and competing anions).  Best removals were achieved in the pH 4 to 9 range depending on the media type. Iron-based medias containing sulfur were very effective at mercury removal (83 to greater than 99 percent). Bentonite clay and turbidity were also shown to remove mercury by adsorption.  In most of the tests, inorganic mercury was used, but sufficent data were not available to conclude anything about the differences between inorganic and organic removal by adsorption.
